source_metadata.title_raw: Terry J. Mollica - I-18-270 - January 24, 2018 - Benicia

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 24, 2018

Terry J. Mollica
Mollica Law
560 First Street, Suite B201
Benicia, CA 94510

Re:    Your Request for Informal Assistance
       Our File No. I-18-270

Dear Mr. Mollica:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. Also, please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

     Additionally, the Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Accordingly, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any past conduct, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

     Moreover, because your inquiry is general in nature and does not contemplate a specific upcoming governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

                                    QUESTION

     May you take part in the Benicia Planning Commission's consideration of cannabis manufacturing licenses given that you have an ownership interest in a cannabis retail business?

                                    CONCLUSION

     No. You should not take part in the Planning Commission's consideration of cannabis manufacturing licenses as the decision regulates an activity in which your business is engaged and it is reasonable foreseeable that the decision will affect the value of your business.

_________________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-18-270
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are one of seven members who sit on the Benicia Planning Commission. Before the
Planning Commission meeting on December 13, 2018 was a matter involving a cannabis use permit
for a cannabis manufacturing facility. On our informal advice, you excused yourself from
participation in that hearing. However, you anticipate future applications for cannabis-related
business licenses in the future, particularly for manufacturing. You stated that cannabis
manufacturers produce oils, extracts, and other products.

The City's cannabis permit program includes different types of licenses. Specifically, the
City accepts public safety licenses applications for cultivation, delivery, distribution, microbusiness,
manufacturing, and retail licenses.³ You stated that each type of public safety license has individual
requirements and regulations affecting each particular type of business.

You hold a minority interest in a company called Green Crossings, which has a pending
application for a cannabis retail license. The City intends to issue only two retail cannabis public
safety licenses. You stated that you intend to recuse yourself from participating in the Planning
Commission's consideration of any applications for a cannabis retail license, whether submitted by
Green Crossings or any other applicant. Green Crossings is not a manufacturer, but may purchase and
resell products from other manufacturers or distributors. Green Crossing has not applied for a
manufacturing license and, to your knowledge, does not intend to do so as of the time of this
request.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Specific to your facts, Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest
may arise, including:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• An interest in a source of income to the official which aggregates to $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)⁴

_______________
³ https://www.ci.benicia.ca.us/cannabis

⁴ While an official also has an interest in his or her personal finances, including those of an immediate family
member (Section 87103), an effect on an official's personal finances is not considered separately where the decision
affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property. (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Therefore, we do not analyze
your interest in your personal finances.

File No. I-18-270
Page No. 3

Based on the provided facts, you have a business interest in Green Crossings, assuming you have a $2,000 or more investment in the business or hold any position of management. Additionally, you have a source of income interest in Green Crossings if you receive income from Green Crossings that aggregates to $500 within 12 months prior to a decision.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Standards for foreseeability vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a decision.⁵ For an interest that is not explicitly involved in a decision at issue, as in this case, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

The standard to determine materiality for a business entity not explicitly involved in the decision is set forth in Regulation 18702.1. Regulation 18702.1(b) provides examples of decisions that may be applicable to determine whether a decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business. Specific to the provided facts, Regulation 18702.1(b)(1) provides that a decision is material where it would "[a]uthorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which the business is engaged."

Under the facts provided, the Planning Commission considers various types of cannabis-related business licenses that authorize, prohibit, or regulate activities in connection with the Benicia cannabis industry, the same business activity in which Green Crossings is engaged. Moreover, Green Crossings has a pending application for one of only two cannabis retail licenses the City intends to issue. Considering the very limited retail market, it is foreseeable that any decision concerning an application to manufacture cannabis goods could affect the value of potential retailers such as Green Crossings.

Accordingly, you should not take part in the Planning Commission's consideration of applications for cannabis manufacturing licenses.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By:    Brian G. Lau
       Assistant General Counsel

SKP:jgl

⁵ An official's interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the interest is a named party in, or subject of, the decision. An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest, or if the decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). (Regulation 18701(a).)

manufacturing licenses given that you have an ownership interest in a cannabis retail business?
